config.task_description: Generate a comic book adaptation of 'Ohio Is Hell on Earth', exploring themes of institutional surveillance, the 'livestock logic' of modern parenting, and the social sorting of secular families in a moral-reform culture.
config.art_style: Gritty Noir with Surrealist Institutional Elements
config.style_details: High contrast, heavy shadows (chiaroscuro). Use clinical white for institutional spaces (offices, schools) and deep, murky shadows for the 'exposed' reality. Visual metaphors should be literal: bureaucrats holding clipboards that look like scythes, children with subtle ear-tags like livestock, and the 'web of mutual recognition' depicted as glowing threads connecting church-aligned families.

character_references.visual_description: Mid-40s, sharp features, wearing glasses and a slightly rumpled button-down shirt. He has the look of a weary intellectual—an AI researcher who has seen too much data. His eyes are tired but observant.
character_references.personality_notes: Analytical, protective, deeply cynical of institutions but deeply loving toward his daughter. He speaks in philosophical observations.
character_references.relationships: Single adoptive father to his daughter; biological father to two sons (seen in flashbacks/photos).

character_references.visual_description: 7 years old, bright and curious. She wears a t-shirt with a T-Rex on it. She often carries a fossil or a science book. She represents the 'illegible' spark of life the system tries to flatten.
character_references.personality_notes: Scientifically literate, oblivious to the bureaucratic weight on her father, but starting to feel the 'remainder' status at school.

character_references.visual_description: An archetypal figure, often seen in silhouette or with features obscured by the glare of office lights. Wears a sharp, dated suit reminiscent of the 1920s Anti-Saloon League era, but with a modern clinical white coat over it.
character_references.personality_notes: Cold, metric-driven, convinced of their own moral righteousness. They don't see people, only 'cases'.
character_references.relationships: Representative of the State of Ohio and the Westerville moral machinery.

plot_continuity.narrative_structure: Five-act structure following the essay's descent into the 'Layers of Hell'.
key_plot_points: The Pediatrician's office: The growth chart is revealed as a livestock metric. Andrew realizes he is being graded on 'yield'.
The Social Topology: A visual map of Westerville showing the 'Protected' (church web), 'Accommodated' (immigrants), and 'Exposed' (Andrew).
The Impossible Father: Andrew in a glass room where his competence is invisible to the bureaucrats outside who only see the 'gap' where a mother should be.
The Sorting: The LifeWise bus arrives at school. The 'saved' children board; the daughter is left behind as a 'remainder' in a cold classroom.
The Ghost of Westerville: The Anti-Saloon League headquarters looming over the town like a panopticon. The 'New Temperance' logic explained.
The Three Layers of Hell: The ambient toxicity, targeted misinterpretation, and ideological offense closing in on Andrew as he stands in a field that is actually a farm.
plot_continuity.setting_details: Westerville, Ohio. A suburb that looks perfect on the surface—manicured lawns, Saturday farmers markets—but feels like a high-pressure cooker or a managed farm from the inside.
continuity_constraints: The daughter's dinosaur shirt must be a recurring visual motif representing her 'dangerous' curiosity.
The 'Anti-Saloon League' imagery should haunt the background of modern settings.
The transition from a medical office to a literal farm should be seamless and surreal.
plot_continuity.ending_notes: The final panel should show the daughter looking at a fossil, defiant in her curiosity, while Andrew looks at the reader, acknowledging that the 'machine' is still running.
plot_continuity.additional_notes: Use James C. Scott's 'Seeing Like a State' as a visual inspiration for how the state 'flattens' reality into grids and numbers.

script.title: Ohio Is Hell on Earth
script.premise: A secular single father navigates the "livestock logic" and moral-reform machinery of Westerville, Ohio, where institutional metrics and religious sorting transform a family into a "case."

characters.name: Andrew
characters.description: Analytical, protective, deeply cynical of institutions. The narrator and father fighting for legibility.
characters.visual_traits: Mid-40s, sharp features, wearing glasses and a slightly rumpled button-down shirt. A weary intellectual with tired but observant eyes.

characters.name: The Daughter
characters.description: Scientifically literate, oblivious to the bureaucracy, but feeling the social "remainder" status. The "output" the state is measuring.
characters.visual_traits: 7 years old, bright and curious. Wears a t-shirt with a T-Rex on it. Often carries a fossil or a science book.

characters.name: The Bureaucrat
characters.description: Cold, metric-driven, morally self-righteous. Representative of the State’s "livestock logic."
characters.visual_traits: Features obscured by the glare of office lights. Wears a 1920s-style suit (Anti-Saloon League era) with a modern clinical white lab coat over it. Carries a clipboard that looks vaguely like a scythe.

frames.description: Extreme close-up of a growth chart. The lines look like a stock market crash. A red dot sits outside the "acceptable" curve.
A clinical white hand holds a pen, circling the outlier dot with aggressive precision.

rows.visual_description: High-contrast white background. The chart looks more like a blueprint for a machine than a medical record.

frames.description: Andrew sits in a small plastic chair, looking up. The Doctor/Bureaucrat is a towering silhouette against a blinding fluorescent light.
Andrew’s daughter sits on the exam table, swinging her legs. She’s wearing her T-Rex shirt. She looks perfectly healthy, but the Doctor is looking only at the chart.
frames.caption: I watched a doctor frown at a growth chart—my daughter’s growth chart—and I realized the concern had nothing to do with her health.

rows.visual_description: Chiaroscuro lighting. Andrew is in deep shadow; the Doctor is a bleached-out white shape.

frames.description: Surreal shift. The office walls peel away like old wallpaper to reveal a vast, muddy cattle feedlot under a grey Ohio sky.
Close-up of the Daughter’s ear. A small, yellow plastic livestock tag is clipped to it. It reads: PERCENTILE: 15th. STATUS: INTERVENTION.
frames.caption: That is livestock logic. A farmer evaluating whether a calf is gaining weight on schedule. The child is not the patient. The child is the output.

rows.visual_description: Gritty, textured mud and rain. The transition from the clean office to the dirty farm is seamless.

frames.description: A map of Westerville, Ohio. It’s being flattened by a giant, transparent grid descending from the sky.
Andrew’s face, half-covered by the grid lines. His features are being converted into a barcode.
frames.caption: The first move of every modern state: make the population legible. Flatten the complexity of life into categories the institution can read.

rows.visual_description: The grid is glowing, clinical blue. It "cuts" through the organic shapes of the town.

frames.description: A "Protected" family. A suburban house wrapped in a glowing web of golden threads. The threads connect to a church steeple and a school board office.
An "Accommodated" family. An immigrant household. They are inside a box labeled "CULTURAL DIFFERENCE (ESL)." The Bureaucrat is checking a box on a form, nodding.

rows.visual_description: The "Protected" web looks like a safety net that also obscures the family from view.

frames.description: Andrew and his daughter standing in an open, empty field. No web, no box. Just a giant, harsh spotlight hitting them from above.
The Bureaucrat stands at the edge of the light, holding a clipboard. He looks at Andrew with a squint of "deficiency."
frames.caption: Then there is the secular local family. Not protected by a church, not legible as "other." Our difference is read as a defect.

rows.visual_description: Andrew looks small and vulnerable. The shadows around the spotlight are pitch black.

frames.description: Andrew is in a glass observation room. He is packing a healthy lunch, helping with homework, and reading a bedtime story.
Outside the glass, a group of Bureaucrats are looking at him. They don't see the lunch or the books. They only see a large, dark silhouette of a woman with a question mark over her face.
frames.caption: A single father is not a category. He is a question. The system cannot see paternal competence. It only sees the empty space where a mother should be.

rows.visual_description: Andrew’s actions are rendered in warm, soft colors, but the glass filters them into cold, grey tones for the observers.

frames.description: A montage of forms. "Mother/Guardian" is highlighted in neon; "Father/Guardian" is a faint afterthought.
A Bureaucrat leans in close to Andrew, his breath visible.

dialog.text: And where... is the mother, Mr. Charneski? Why are you the one here?

rows.visual_description: The Bureaucrat’s face is a mask of "clinical concern" that looks like an interrogation.

frames.description: A manila folder slams shut. The label reads: CHARNESKI, A. – SITUATION TO BE MANAGED.
frames.caption: The data said "competent father." The architecture said "situation." The architecture won every time.

rows.visual_description: The folder is thick, bursting with papers that look like they’re trying to escape.

frames.description: A bright yellow school bus pulls up. It has "LIFEWISE ACADEMY" written on the side in a font that looks like a hymn book.
Most of the children in the classroom stand up, glowing with a soft, communal light. They board the bus together.

rows.visual_description: The children boarding the bus look like a single, unified organism.

frames.description: Andrew’s daughter sits alone in a cavernous, dark classroom. The desks around her are empty. The clock on the wall is ticking loudly.
She looks down at her T-Rex shirt. The dinosaur looks lonely.
frames.caption: The LifeWise kids return as a group. My daughter is not part of a group. She is a remainder. She is what is left when the community leaves the room.

rows.visual_description: The classroom is tinted in a cold, lonely blue. The shadows of the empty desks stretch toward her.

frames.description: A group of "Saved" children stand in a circle, whispering and pointing at the Daughter.

dialog.text: She believes in the old earth.
My mom says her house is... dangerous.

frames.description: Andrew’s daughter holds a small trilobite fossil, her knuckles white.
frames.caption: Her scientific curiosity is treated as evidence of abuse. My fitness is evaluated through the lens of a community I do not belong to.

rows.visual_description: The whispering children are drawn with slightly exaggerated, judgmental features.

frames.description: The Anti-Saloon League headquarters in Westerville. A grim, stone building that looks like a fortress.
A ghost-like figure in a 1920s top hat and coat stands in the window, looking out over the modern suburb.
frames.caption: Westerville calls itself the "Dry Capital of the World." It’s a boast. The civic DNA here is moral reform. Surveillance in the name of protection.

rows.visual_description: The building looms over the modern houses like a panopticon.

frames.description: A modern kitchen. A pressure cooker sits on the stove, whistling violently.
The Bureaucrat is taking notes on the steam.
frames.caption: The new temperance uses the school schedule to remove the secular from the social body. Building a pressure cooker and pathologizing the steam.

rows.visual_description: The steam from the cooker forms the shape of a DNA double helix, which the Bureaucrat is "measuring" with a ruler.

frames.description: A neighbor peeks through closed blinds, holding a phone. Their face is twisted in "righteous concern."
A CPS worker’s car idles at the curb of Andrew’s house. The headlights look like predatory eyes.
frames.caption: The enforcers are not aberrations. They are the immune system of the enclosure.

rows.visual_description: High-contrast noir. The neighborhood looks like a prison camp disguised as a cul-de-sac.

frames.description: Andrew is walking down a staircase that descends into a dark pit. The walls are made of filing cabinets.
Whispers emerge from the cabinets: "Unstable... Secular... Situation... Outlier..."
frames.caption: Layer one: Ambient toxicity. Layer two: Targeted misinterpretation. Layer three: Ideological offense.

rows.visual_description: The filing cabinets are leaking black ink that pools at Andrew’s feet.

frames.description: Andrew and his daughter stand in their backyard. The wooden fence has transformed into barbed wire. A watchtower looms where the neighbor’s oak tree used to be.
Andrew looks at the reader. His glasses are cracked. He looks exhausted but defiant.

dialog.text: Are we planning on eating the kids? No. We’re just processing them.

rows.visual_description: The "suburb" is now fully revealed as a managed farm. The lighting is the grey, oppressive light of a permanent Ohio winter.

frames.description: Close-up of the Daughter’s hand. She is holding a fossilized dinosaur tooth. It is sharp and real.
Wide shot. Andrew and the Daughter stand together as the "machine"—a giant, mechanical Bureaucrat made of clipboards and gears—looms over them.
frames.caption: My daughter believes in dinosaurs. The system believes this is a problem. One of us is right. This is Ohio. The heart of it all. That is exactly where it hurts.

rows.visual_description: The daughter looks at the fossil with a small, secret smile. Andrew stares down the machine. Blackout.
